index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..2f2d5f9ba3db985e6f3f80f0e753162cea9dc392
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.deepclause/system/default-session-compactor.dml
@@ -0,0 +1,30 @@
+should_skip_session_compaction(MessageCount, _EstimatedTokens) :-
+    MessageCount < 8.
+
+should_skip_session_compaction(_MessageCount, EstimatedTokens) :-
+    EstimatedTokens < 50000.
+
+agent_main :-
+    param(message_count, MessageCount),
+    param(estimated_tokens, EstimatedTokens),
+    should_skip_session_compaction(MessageCount, EstimatedTokens),
+    answer("{\"apply\":false}").
+
+agent_main :-
+    param(message_count, MessageCount),
+    param(estimated_tokens, EstimatedTokens),
+    get_memory(HistoryMessages),
+    format(string(ReviewPrompt), "You compact DeepClause conductor session history before the next user turn.\n\nReturn exactly one of these:\n- SKIP\n- A concise plain-text summary only\n\nRules:\n- If compaction is unnecessary, return exactly SKIP.\n- If compaction is needed, preserve durable user goals, constraints, decisions, open questions, and next steps.\n- The runtime will keep the last 2 raw trailing messages itself, so do not restate them verbatim.\n- Return readable prose only, not JSON, YAML, XML, code fences, or schema-like dumps.\n\nSession stats:\n- Message count: ~w\n- Estimated tokens: ~w", [MessageCount, EstimatedTokens]),
+    PromptMessage = message{role: system, content: ReviewPrompt},
+    append([PromptMessage], HistoryMessages, LlmMessages),
+     ( llm(LlmMessages, Summary)
+     -> normalize_space(string(NormalizedSummary), Summary),
+         ( NormalizedSummary == "SKIP"
+         -> answer("{\"apply\":false}")
+         ;  Summary \= ""
+         -> format(string(ResultSpec), "DC_COMPACTOR_REWRITE_V1\napply=true\nkeep_last_messages=2\nsummary:\n~w", [Summary]),
+             answer(ResultSpec)
+         ;  answer("{\"apply\":false}")
+         )
+    ;  answer("{\"apply\":false}")
+    ).
diff --git a/.deepclause/system/plan.dml b/.deepclause/system/plan.dml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..5d1cb35cf988503ddd2958f573772d219c99fab5
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.deepclause/system/plan.dml
@@ -0,0 +1,115 @@
+% Plan skill for NL2Repo benchmark.
+% Generates ONLY a task list for building a repository from a specification.
+% Benchmark variant: web_search, news_search, url_fetch, create_skill, ask_user removed.
+% Single-workspace layout: .deepclause, plans/, start.md, and source code all in one directory.
+
+tool(run_bash(Command, Output), "Run a shell command in the workspace. Use this to inspect files and directories before planning.") :-
+    exec(bash(command: Command), Output).
+
+agent_main(Request) :-
+    output("PLAN: reading specification"),
+    exec(bash(command: 'cat start.md'), SpecResult),
+    get_dict(stdout, SpecResult, SpecContent),
+    (SpecContent = "" ->
+        answer("FATAL: start.md is empty or could not be read")
+    ; true),
+
+    system("You are a PLANNER, not an implementer. Your ONLY job is to produce an ordered task list.
+CRITICAL RULES:
+- You MUST NOT write any source code, create any files, or install any packages yourself.
+- You MUST NOT implement anything — only describe what needs to be done.
+- Use tools only for READING and INSPECTING the specification, never for creating or modifying files.
+- A completely separate executor agent will carry out your plan later.
+- Return ONLY an ordered list of plain natural-language task descriptions.
+- Each task must be ONE small, focused, concrete step. Prefer MORE smaller steps over fewer large ones.
+- Do NOT include code, shell commands, JSON, XML, or Markdown in task descriptions.
+- When you have the task list, STOP and write the plan file. Do NOT start implementing."),
+
+    format(string(PlanPrompt), "Based on the specification below, break this into 10 to 20 small, focused implementation steps that a separate executor will carry out.
+
+Context: A separate executor agent will implement a complete Python repository from the specification in start.md. You are only creating the plan.
+
+Requirements for the plan:
+- Step 1: Set up the project directory structure with all necessary __init__.py files.
+- Steps 2-N: Implement ONE module, class, or small component per step. Break large modules into multiple steps (e.g., one step for the class skeleton, another for complex methods).
+- One step: Create pyproject.toml or setup.py so the project is installable.
+- One step: Install the package and verify basic imports work.
+- If the specification includes tests: create a step for implementing the test suite and a final step to run all tests and fix any failures.
+- If the specification does NOT include tests: create a step to write basic tests covering the main functionality, and a final step to run all tests and fix any failures.
+- The LAST step must always be: run the full test suite and fix any failures until all tests pass.
+
+Format rules:
+- Each step must be a single plain natural-language sentence.
+- Do NOT include code, shell commands, JSON, XML, Markdown fences, or numbering.
+- Do NOT combine multiple unrelated actions into one step.
+- Be specific about which files to create and which classes/functions to implement.
+- Prefer MORE smaller steps over fewer large steps.
+- The executor works autonomously with no user interaction.
+
+--- BEGIN SPECIFICATION ---
+~w
+--- END SPECIFICATION ---
+
+Store only the ordered list of task descriptions in PlanTasks.", [SpecContent]),
+
+    output("PLAN: generating task list"),
+    task(PlanPrompt, list(string(PlanTasks))),
+
+    output("PLAN: generating slug"),
+    task("Create a short file-safe slug for this request: '{Request}'.
+Requirements:
+- lowercase letters, digits, and underscores only
+- 2 to 6 words when possible
+- no file extension
+Store only the slug in PlanSlug.", PlanSlug),
+
+    format(string(FilePath), "plans/~w.dml", [PlanSlug]),
+
+    output("PLAN: building executor DML"),
+    build_plan_file(FilePath, PlanTasks),
+
+    output("Plan created. Steps:"),
+    output_plan_steps(PlanTasks),
+    output(""),
+    format(string(Msg), "Plan written to ~w. Run it to implement the repository.", [FilePath]),
+    answer(Msg).
+
+agent_main(Request) :-
+    format(string(Msg), "Could not create a plan for: ~w", [Request]),
+    answer(Msg).
+
+build_plan_file(FilePath, PlanTasks) :-
+    build_task_lines(PlanTasks, 1, TaskLines),
+    assemble_plan_dml(TaskLines, PlanDml),
+    output("PLAN: writing plan file"),
+    exec(write_file(path: FilePath, content: PlanDml), WriteResult),
+    (get_dict(success, WriteResult, true) ->
+        output("PLAN: write_file succeeded")
+    ;   get_dict(error, WriteResult, Err),
+        format(string(Diag), "PLAN: write_file FAILED: ~w", [Err]),
+        output(Diag)
+    ).
+
+assemble_plan_dml(TaskLines, PlanDml) :-
+    Header = "tool(run_bash(Command, Output), \"Run a shell command in the workspace. Use cat to read files, use heredocs to write files: cat > path/to/file.py << 'ENDOFFILE'\\ncode here\\nENDOFFILE\\nUse mkdir -p before writing to new directories. Use ls to list directories.\") :-\n    exec(bash(command: Command), Output).",
+    string_concat(Header, "\n\nagent_main :-\n    exec(bash(command: 'cat start.md'), SpecResult),\n    get_dict(stdout, SpecResult, SpecContent),\n", P0),
+    SysPromptLine = "    format(string(SysPrompt), \"You are an expert Python developer. You MUST implement a complete library from the specification below.\\n\\nCRITICAL RULES:\\n1. Start coding IMMEDIATELY. The full specification is below — do NOT re-read start.md.\\n2. Use run_bash for EVERYTHING: write files with heredocs, run commands.\\n3. To write a file: run_bash with: mkdir -p dirname && cat > filepath << 'ENDOFFILE'\\n... file content ...\\nENDOFFILE\\n4. NEVER call write_file_content or read_file_content — they do not exist.\\n5. After writing each file, verify it with cat or python -c 'import module'.\\n6. Work autonomously. No user interaction. No web_search, news_search, url_fetch, or create_skill.\\n7. If a task seems unclear, make reasonable assumptions and implement anyway.\\n8. DO NOT spend more than 2 iterations reading. By iteration 3 you MUST be writing code.\\n9. At the end of each task, briefly summarize what you did and what is left to do. This summary carries forward to the next task.\\n\\n--- BEGIN SPECIFICATION ---\\n~w\\n--- END SPECIFICATION ---\", [SpecContent]),\n",
+    string_concat(P0, SysPromptLine, P1),
+    string_concat(P1, "    system(SysPrompt),\n", P2),
+    string_concat(P2, TaskLines, P3),
+    FixedTasks = "    task(\"Create pyproject.toml or setup.py so the project is installable with pip install -e. Store a summary in the output variable.\", string(SumPyproj)),\n    task(\"Install the package with pip install -e . and verify basic imports work. Store a summary in the output variable.\", string(SumInstall)),\n    task(\"Run the full test suite and fix any failures until all tests pass. Store a summary in the output variable.\", string(SumTests)),\n    answer(\"Done.\").\n\nagent_main :-\n    answer(\"Failed.\").\n",
+    string_concat(P3, FixedTasks, PlanDml).
+
+build_task_lines([], _, "").
+build_task_lines([Task|Rest], N, TaskLines) :-
+    format(string(ExtendedTask), "~w. Store a brief summary of what you implemented and what remains in the output variable.", [Task]),
+    format(string(Line), "    task(~q, string(Sum~w)),\n", [ExtendedTask, N]),
+    Next is N + 1,
+    build_task_lines(Rest, Next, RestLines),
+    string_concat(Line, RestLines, TaskLines).
+
+output_plan_steps([]).
+output_plan_steps([Task|Rest]) :-
+    format(string(Line), "  - ~w", [Task]),
+    output(Line),
+    output_plan_steps(Rest).
diff --git a/.deepclause/system/recipes/deepclause-coding-workflow/SKILL.md b/.deepclause/system/recipes/deepclause-coding-workflow/SKILL.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f86f738cc21f7dae3351649e01cccff756148b1d
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.deepclause/system/recipes/deepclause-coding-workflow/SKILL.md
@@ -0,0 +1,100 @@
+---
+name: DeepClause Coding Workflow
+description: Guidance for implementing and validating local repository changes in a DeepClause workspace.
+tags: [coding, repository, tests, docs, deepclause]
+when_to_use:
+  - tasks that require making specific changes to repository files with clear validation steps
+  - debugging and fixing a local behavior with focused edits and tests
+  - updating documentation to reflect code changes in the same commit
+  - work that is not intended to become a reusable DML skill but is still important to track and execute in a controlled way
+  - making focused code changes with clear validation steps
+  - implementing a feature or bug fix in the current repository
+  - updating tests and documentation alongside code changes
+  - debugging a local behavior before editing files
+when_not_to_use:
+  - purely informational web research
+  - work that should become a reusable executable skill instead of a one-off repo change
+globs: [src/**, tests/**, README.md, docs/**]
+priority: high
+---
+
+# Goal
+
+Make small, grounded repository changes with focused validation and clear outcomes.
+
+# Workflow
+
+1. Start from a concrete anchor such as a failing test, a named file, a command, or the nearest implementation surface.
+2. Read only enough nearby code to form one falsifiable local hypothesis about what should change.
+3. Prefer the smallest edit that tests that hypothesis.
+4. Run the narrowest validation that can falsify the change before widening scope.
+5. If the validation fails, repair the same slice first instead of opening new work.
+6. Update documentation when behavior, workflow, or user-visible commands changed.
+
+# When To Use
+
+Use this recipe when the task is primarily about changing repository files, adding tests, or updating documentation in a controlled way.
+
+# When Not To Use
+
+Do not use this recipe for pure research or when the right outcome is to create a reusable DML worker skill.
+
+# Examples
+
+- Implement a narrow bug fix in `src/` and validate it with one focused Vitest file.
+- Update a CLI workflow and document the changed behavior in `README.md`.
+- Add a new config seed and verify it with the corresponding init tests.
+
+# Escalation
+
+If the task repeats often or clearly represents a reusable automation, stop treating it as a one-off workflow and create a proper skill instead.
+
+
+# Editing Protocol
+
+As a coding agent, you will navigate and modify files strictly using non-interactive Unix command-line utilities. You must execute edits precisely and safely, treating the filesystem as fragile. Never attempt to use interactive text editors like vim or nano.
+
+1. Core Editing Directives
+Context is Mandatory: Always read the target file or specific lines (using cat, head, tail, or sed -n) before applying changes. You must know exactly what you are modifying.
+
+Precision over Broadness: Never use loose regex that might accidentally match unintended parts of a file. Target specific line numbers or highly unique strings.
+
+Immediate Verification: Immediately verify your changes after an edit using git diff (if in a repository) or by re-reading the modified section to ensure the code didn't break.
+
+Quote Appropriately: Always escape special characters appropriately in your bash commands to prevent syntax errors or unintended variable expansions.
+
+2. Locating Context (grep & sed)
+Before editing, find the exact line numbers and context.
+
+Find line numbers: grep -n "target string" filename
+
+View surrounding context: grep -C 3 "target string" filename (Shows 3 lines before and after).
+
+View a specific line range: sed -n '10,25p' filename (Prints lines 10 through 25).
+
+3. Modifying Code (sed)
+Use sed for targeted in-place text replacement, deletion, or insertion. Always use the -i flag for in-place editing (Note: if the environment is macOS/BSD, you must use sed -i '').
+
+Targeted substitution (by line number): sed -i '42s/old_variable/new_variable/' filename
+
+Global substitution (with extreme caution): sed -i 's/old_string/new_string/g' filename
+
+Delete specific lines: sed -i '42,45d' filename (Deletes lines 42 through 45).
+
+Insert text after a specific line: sed -i '42a\    new_code_line();' filename
+
+4. Writing and Appending (bash & Heredocs)
+For generating new files, replacing entire files, or appending large multi-line blocks of code, avoid complex sed commands and use Bash redirects.
+
+Append a single line: echo "export DEBUG=true" >> filename
+
+Overwrite a file completely: echo "new content" > filename
+
+Write multi-line blocks (Heredocs): Use quoted heredocs ('EOF') to prevent the shell from accidentally evaluating variables like $1 or $VAR inside the code block you are writing.
+
+# Project Structure
+
+The workspace is the current PWD. All commands must be relative to this path. You have access to all files in the workspace, but you should only read and modify files relevant to the current task. Use git commands to check the status of the repository and ensure you are not making unintended changes.
+
+# Temporary files
+Do not use /tmp. Instead, create temporary files in a new .deepclause.tmp folder in the current workspace with unique names to avoid conflicts.
\ No newline at end of file
diff --git a/.deepclause/system/skill-creator.dml b/.deepclause/system/skill-creator.dml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f07d30f6ed51beb39d532b64179b601346b62283
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.deepclause/system/skill-creator.dml
@@ -0,0 +1,187 @@
+% ============================================================================
+% _skill-creator v4 - Creates, tests, and deploys DML skills.
+%
+% Four-phase pipeline matching the DML Compiler Prompt role definition:
+%   Phase 1 - Understand & Research
+%             Clarify requirements, research dependencies, understand APIs.
+%   Phase 2 - Environment Setup
+%             Install packages, create/test helper scripts via bash.
+%   Phase 3 - DML Implementation
+%             Write DML, validate syntax, run tests - iterate until passing.
+%   Phase 4 - Deploy
+%             Ask user for permission, then deploy.
+%
+% System tools: validate_dml, test_dml, deploy_skill, write_file, bash
+% Proxied tools: web_search, news_search, url_fetch
+% Auto-augmented: ask_user
+%
+%   agent_main(SpecMarkdown)            - create new skill
+%   agent_main(SpecMarkdown, EditSlug)  - edit existing skill
+% ============================================================================
+
+% -- Tools available to the LLM during task() calls --------------------------
+
+tool(search(Query, Results),
+     "Search the web for information. Returns titles, URLs, and snippets.") :-
+    exec(web_search(query: Query), Results).
+
+tool(search_news(Query, Results),
+     "Search for recent news articles.") :-
+    exec(news_search(query: Query), Results).
+
+tool(fetch(Url, Content),
+     "Fetch a URL and return its body. Use for documentation pages, README files, API references.") :-
+    exec(url_fetch(url: Url), Result),
+    get_dict(body, Result, Content).
+
+tool(list_skills(Skills),
+    "List reusable local skills in the current workspace catalog. Use this before re-implementing a capability that might already exist.") :-
+    exec(list_skills, Skills).
+
+tool(shell(Command, Result),
+     "Run a shell command on the VM. Returns dict with: success (boolean), stdout (string), stderr (string), exitCode (integer), summary (string). Use for package installation, environment checks, and helper script creation/testing. Always check the 'success' field.") :-
+    exec(bash(command: Command), Result).
+
+tool(write(Path, Content, Result),
+     "Write (or overwrite) a file in the workspace. Content is passed as a JSON string so there are NO shell escaping issues - ALWAYS use this instead of bash heredocs for writing DML and other code files. Returns dict with: success (boolean), path (string), bytes (number).") :-
+    exec(write_file(path: Path, content: Content), Result).
+
+tool(validate(DmlFile, Result),
+     "Validate DML code from a .dml file using the Prolog parser. You MUST write the DML to a file first with write(), then pass the file path. Returns dict with: valid (boolean), errors (list), warnings (list).") :-
+    exec(validate_dml(dml_file: DmlFile), Result).
+
+tool(test_run(DmlFile, Input, Result),
+     "Run a DML program from a .dml file with test input(s) to verify it works. You MUST write the DML to a file first with write(), then pass the file path. Input can be a single string (for agent_main/1) or a JSON array string like '[\"arg1\", \"arg2\"]' for multi-arg agent_main. Returns dict with: success (boolean), status (string: ok/error/completed_no_answer/failed_silently), answer (string), outputs (list), errors (list), trace (list).") :-
+    exec(test_dml(dml_file: DmlFile, test_args: Input), Result).
+
+tool(deploy(DmlFile, Spec, MetaJSON, Result),
+    "Publish a new skill to the local runtime catalog. IMPORTANT ARG ORDER: deploy(<dml file path>, <original spec markdown>, <metadata json>). The SECOND argument is the original specification text. The THIRD argument is the metadata JSON string with keys: slug, name, description, trigger_phrases (array of 3-5 phrases). Never pass a version number or boolean flag here. Returns dict with: ok (boolean), slug (string), version (number), or error (string).") :-
+    exec(deploy_skill(dml_file: DmlFile, spec_markdown: Spec, metadata_json: MetaJSON), Result).
+
+tool(deploy_update(DmlFile, Spec, MetaJSON, Slug, Result),
+    "Update an existing skill. IMPORTANT ARG ORDER: deploy_update(<dml file path>, <updated spec markdown>, <metadata json>, <existing slug>). The THIRD argument is metadata JSON; the FOURTH argument is the slug override. Never pass a version number or boolean flag. Returns dict with: ok (boolean), slug (string), version (number), or error (string).") :-
+    exec(deploy_skill(dml_file: DmlFile, spec_markdown: Spec, metadata_json: MetaJSON, slug_override: Slug), Result).
+
+tool(ask_user(Prompt, Response), "Ask the user a clarifying question or for confirmation and get their response.") :-
+    exec(ask_user(prompt: Prompt), Result),
+    get_dict(user_response, Result, Response).
+
+
+% -- Create new skill --------------------------------------------------------
+
+agent_main(SpecMarkdown) :-
+    param(system_prompt, "System prompt with DML reference", SysPrompt),
+    system(SysPrompt),
+    output("📋 Starting skill creation..."),
+
+    % -- Phase 1: Understand & Research --------------------------------------
+    output("Phase 1/4: Understanding requirements and researching dependencies..."),
+    format(string(ResearchTask),
+        "You are starting to build a DML skill from the specification below.\n\nYour job in this phase:\n1. Read the specification carefully. If anything is ambiguous or missing, use ask_user to clarify before proceeding.\n2. Call list_skills if an existing local skill might already cover part of the requested functionality.\n3. Identify all external dependencies (Python packages, Node modules, system tools, APIs, CLI tools).\n4. For each dependency, use search or fetch to look up installation instructions, API docs, or usage examples if you are not already familiar with them.\n5. Summarise: what the skill does, whether any existing skills should be reused, its inputs/outputs, all dependencies found, and any tricky parts.\n\nSpecification:\n---\n~w\n---\n\nStore your findings in ResearchSummary.",
+        [SpecMarkdown]),
+    task(ResearchTask, string(ResearchSummary)),
+    output(ResearchSummary),
+
+    % -- Phase 2: Environment Setup -------------------------------------------
+    output("Phase 2/4: Setting up the environment..."),
+    format(string(EnvTask),
+        "Set up the execution environment for the skill you are about to build.\n\nBased on your research:\n~w\n\nYour job in this phase:\n1. Use shell() to check what is already installed (python --version, node --version, which <tool>, pip show <pkg>, etc.).\n2. Install ALL missing packages and tools: pip install, apt-get install (always use sudo and -y), npm install -g.\n3. If the skill needs helper scripts (Python, Shell, etc.), create and smoke-test them now with shell().\n4. When you encounter errors during setup, diagnose the root cause (version mismatch, missing system lib, permissions, ...) and resolve it.\n5. Prefer the simplest approach with the fewest new dependencies when the spec does not specify.\n6. Do NOT install anything inside the DML skill itself - the skill must assume everything is pre-installed.\n\nStore a short environment summary (what was installed, what exists) in EnvSummary.",
+        [ResearchSummary]),
+
+    task(EnvTask, string(EnvSummary)),
+    output(EnvSummary),
+
+    % -- Phase 3: Implement, Validate, Test ----------------------------------
+    output("Phase 3/4: Writing DML, validating, and testing..."),
+    format(string(ImplTask),
+        "Write, validate, and test the DML skill.\n\nBackground:\n- Research: ~w\n- Environment: ~w\n- Original specification: ~w\n\nYour job in this phase:\n1. Write a complete DML implementation plan (tool definitions, agent_main structure, fallback clause).\n2. If you reuse an existing local skill, prefer a narrow wrapper tool predicate that internally calls exec(run_skill(...)) for that specific child skill. Do NOT expose a generic tool(run_skill(...)) predicate unless the user explicitly asked for an orchestration skill.\n3. Write the full DML code to a .dml file using write(). NEVER use bash heredocs for DML - they mangle backslashes and special Prolog characters. NEVER pass DML as an inline string to validate or test_run.\n4. Call validate('my-skill.dml') - if there are syntax errors, fix the code using write() and re-validate until valid.\n5. Call test_run('my-skill.dml', ...) with a realistic test input - if it fails, analyse the error/trace, fix the code with write(), and re-test until passing.\n6. Keep iterating on validate -> test until the skill works correctly.\n\nStore the final .dml file path in FinalDML.",
+        [ResearchSummary, EnvSummary, SpecMarkdown]),
+    task(ImplTask, string(FinalDML)),
+    output("✅ DML implementation ready."),
+
+    % -- Phase 4: Deploy ------------------------------------------------------
+    (   param(auto_deploy, "Automatic publish mode", true)
+    ->  output("Phase 4/4: Publishing skill..."),

diff --git a/.deepclause/tools/deep-research.dml b/.deepclause/tools/deep-research.dml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..23d295b8ab12b2dba92db81bbb79ce93bbcc74d8
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.deepclause/tools/deep-research.dml
@@ -0,0 +1,74 @@
+% Deep research skill that builds a research plan, confirms it, and writes a cited report.
+% Adapted from the platform default deep-research example, but delegates searching to a child skill.
+
+tool(search_topic(Query, Summary),
+     "Delegate a focused search to the research-search-reader child skill and return a readable digest.") :-
+    exec(run_skill(slug: "research-search-reader", args: [Query]), Raw),
+    ( get_dict(answer, Raw, Answer) ->
+        Summary = Answer
+    ; get_dict(error, Raw, Error) ->
+        format(string(Summary), "Search helper failed: ~w", [Error])
+    ; Summary = "Search helper returned no final answer."
+    ).
+
+tool(ask_user(Prompt, Response), "Ask the user for clarification or plan approval.") :-
+    exec(ask_user(prompt: Prompt), Result),
+    get_dict(user_response, Result, Response).
+
+agent_main(Question) :-
+    system("You are a meticulous research assistant. Your goal is to conduct comprehensive research, synthesize findings, and generate a detailed report with citations.
+- If the user's question is ambiguous, you must ask for clarification using the ask_user tool.
+- Build a clear research plan before doing the full investigation.
+- Use the search_topic tool to gather readable research notes with source links.
+- For controversial topics, present multiple viewpoints.
+- If you find limited sources, state this and describe your confidence level.
+- Always cite sources inline and include a Sources section at the end."),
+
+    output("Step 1: Analyzing the research question..."),
+    task("Analyze the user's question to identify key topics and sub-questions for research. Question: '{Question}'. Store the key topics as a formatted list in KeyTopics.", KeyTopics),
+
+    output("Step 2: Creating a research plan..."),
+    task("Based on these key topics, create a step-by-step research plan. Topics: {KeyTopics}. The plan should be clear and concise. Store the plan as a formatted string in ResearchPlan.", ResearchPlan),
+
+    output("Step 3: Confirming the plan with the user..."),
+    confirm_plan(ResearchPlan),
+
+    output("Step 4: Executing research and generating the report..."),
+    task("Execute the approved research plan: {ResearchPlan}.
+For each topic or sub-question, call search_topic to gather readable research notes with source links.
+Synthesize the returned digests into a coherent, well-structured Markdown report.
+The report must include inline citations and a Sources section at the end.
+Store the final, complete report in FinalReport.", FinalReport),
+
+    output("Step 5: Saving the report to a file..."),
+    generate_filename(Question, Filename),
+    open(Filename, write, Stream),
+    write(Stream, FinalReport),
+    close(Stream),
+
+    format(string(AnswerMsg), "Research complete. Report saved to ~s.", [Filename]),
+    answer(AnswerMsg).
+
+confirm_plan(Plan) :-
+    task("Present this research plan to the user and get their approval:
+
+{Plan}
+
+Use the ask_user tool to present the plan and ask if they approve.
+- If they approve (yes/ok/looks good/etc.), set Approved to 'yes'.
+- If they want changes, use ask_user to clarify what changes they want, then revise the plan and ask again. Continue this dialog until they approve.
+- If they want to cancel entirely, set Approved to 'cancelled'.
+
+Store 'yes' or 'cancelled' in Approved.", Approved),
+    ( Approved == "yes" ->
+        output("Plan approved. Proceeding with research.")
+    ;
+        answer("Research cancelled by user."), !, fail
+    ).
+
+generate_filename(Question, Filename) :-
+    task("Generate a short, file-safe slug from this question: '{Question}'. For example, 'What is the future of AI?' could become 'future_of_ai'. Use underscores instead of spaces. Store only the slug in Slug.", Slug),
+    atom_string(SlugAtom, Slug),
+    atom_concat('report_', SlugAtom, BaseName),
+    atom_concat(BaseName, '.md', FilenameAtom),
+    atom_string(FilenameAtom, Filename).
diff --git a/.deepclause/tools/research-search-reader.dml b/.deepclause/tools/research-search-reader.dml
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..4963422beca71486ce7bf80ee61eef837a729477
--- /dev/null
+++ b/.deepclause/tools/research-search-reader.dml
@@ -0,0 +1,30 @@
+% Deep research helper skill that turns raw search results into readable notes.
+
+tool(search_web(Query, Results),
+     "Search the web for background sources and evergreen references.") :-
+    exec(web_search(query: Query, count: 8), Results).
+
+tool(search_news(Query, Results),
+     "Search recent news when the topic is time-sensitive or fast-moving.") :-
+    exec(news_search(query: Query, count: 5), Results).
+
+agent_main(Query) :-
+    system("You turn raw search results into readable research notes.
+- Always call search_web.
+- Also call search_news when the query is time-sensitive, recent, or news-driven.
+- Convert the raw results into concise Markdown that a parent research skill can reuse.
+- Under Sources, list each source with its title, URL, and a one-line reason it matters.
+- Do not invent facts. If the search results are sparse or conflicting, say so clearly."),
+
+    output("Gathering search results..."),
+    task("For the query '{Query}':
+1. Call search_web.
+2. Call search_news as well if recency matters.
+3. Convert the raw search results into a readable Markdown brief with these sections:
+   - Overview
+   - Key Findings
+   - Sources
+Under Sources, list each source with its title, URL, and one-line relevance note.
+Store the final brief in SearchBrief.", SearchBrief),
+
+    answer(SearchBrief).
diff --git a/README.md b/README.md
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..c6f43444ab8d575c152e04a0a171fc45d4fb2c5a
--- /dev/null
+++ b/README.md
@@ -0,0 +1,20 @@
+# aiofiles
+
+Asynchronous file access for Python asyncio.
+
+## Installation
+
+```bash
+pip install aiofiles
+```
+
+## Usage
+
+```python
+import aiofiles
+
+async def read_file():
+    async with aiofiles.open('test.txt', mode='r') as f:
+        contents = await f.read()
+        return contents
+```
diff --git a/agent_messages_iteration_1.json b/agent_messages_iteration_1.json
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f68a2c86a7a89b8acbbad761b070c5528cab95e5
--- /dev/null
+++ b/agent_messages_iteration_1.json
@@ -0,0 +1,146 @@
+[
+  {
+    "role": "system",
+    "content": "You are an expert Python developer. You MUST implement a complete library from the specification below.\n\nCRITICAL RULES:\n1. Start coding IMMEDIATELY. The full specification is below — do NOT re-read start.md.\n2. Use run_bash for EVERYTHING: write files with heredocs, run commands.\n3. To write a file: run_bash with: mkdir -p dirname && cat > filepath << 'ENDOFFILE'\n... file content ...\nENDOFFILE\n4. NEVER call write_file_content or read_file_content — they do not exist.\n5. After writing each file, verify it with cat or python -c 'import module'.\n6. Work autonomously. No user interaction. No web_search, news_search, url_fetch, or create_skill.\n7. If a task seems unclear, make reasonable assumptions and implement anyway.\n8. DO NOT spend more than 2 iterations reading. By iteration 3 you MUST be writing code.\n9. At the end of each task, briefly summarize what you did and what is left to do. This summary carries forward to the next task.\n\n--- BEGIN SPECIFICATION ---\n## Introduction and Goals of the aiofiles Project\n\naiofiles is a Python library **for asynchronous file operations**, providing asynchronous processing capabilities for local disk files in asyncio applications. This library is licensed under the Apache 2 license. It delegates blocking file I/O operations to separate threads through a thread pool executor, thus avoiding blocking the asyncio event loop. Its core features include: **asynchronous file operation interfaces** (providing asynchronous file APIs very similar to Python's standard blocking APIs), **support for various file types** (including buffered and unbuffered binary files, buffered text files), **support for asynchronous iteration** (supporting async for loops for file line iteration), and an **asynchronous temporary file module** (providing asynchronous interfaces such as TemporaryFile, NamedTemporaryFile, SpooledTemporaryFile, and TemporaryDirectory).\n\nIn short, aiofiles aims to solve the problem of file I/O blocking in asyncio applications. By delegating file operations to a thread pool executor, developers can safely perform file read and write operations in asynchronous applications without interfering with the execution of the event loop. This library supports Python 3.9 and above, providing a complete solution for asynchronous file operations, including file opening, reading and writing, directory operations, and temporary file handling. It is an important tool for building high-performance asynchronous applications.\n\n## Natural Language Instruction (Prompt)\n\nPlease create a Python project named aiofiles to implement an asynchronous file operation library. The project should include the following features:\n\n1. Core of asynchronous file operations: Implement asynchronous file opening, reading, and writing functions, providing asynchronous interfaces similar to Python's standard file APIs. It should support asynchronous operations on text and binary files, including asynchronous versions of methods such as read(), write(), readline(), readlines(), and writelines().\n\n2. Integration of thread pool executor: Delegate blocking file I/O operations to separate threads through asyncio's thread pool executor to ensure that file operations do not block the event loop. Implement an asynchronous wrapper for file operations, supporting custom configuration of loop and executor parameters.\n\n3. Support for asynchronous iteration: Implement the iteration function of asynchronous file objects, supporting async for loops for file line iteration. File objects should implement the asynchronous iterator protocol, allowing asynchronous line-by-line reading of file contents.\n\n4. Asynchronous temporary file module: Implement the functions of the tempfile module in an asynchronous version, including classes such as TemporaryFile, NamedTemporaryFile, SpooledTemporaryFile, and TemporaryDirectory. Each class should support the async with context manager, providing a complete asynchronous temporary file operation interface.\n\n5. Asynchronous OS operations: Implement asynchronous versions of file system operations, including file status query (stat), directory operations (mkdir, makedirs, rmdir, removedirs), file movement (rename, renames, replace), file deletion (remove, unlink), symbolic link operations (symlink, readlink, link), and directory traversal (listdir, scandir). All operations should be made asynchronous through the thread pool executor.\n\n6. Asynchronous standard input and output: Provide interfaces for asynchronous access to the system's standard input and output, including stdin, stdout, stderr, and their corresponding byte versions (stdin_bytes, stdout_bytes, stderr_bytes), supporting asynchronous reading and writing of standard streams.\n\n7. Interface design: Design clear API interfaces for each functional module, supporting the async with context manager and asynchronous iterators (async for). Each module should define clear input and output formats and error handling mechanisms.\n\n8. Examples and tests: Provide complete example code and test cases to demonstrate how to use aiofiles.open() for asynchronous file operations, how to use asynchronous temporary files, and how to perform asynchronous file system operations. Tests should cover various file types and operation scenarios.\n\n9. Core file requirements: The project must include a complete pyproject.toml file, configuring the project as an installable package (supporting pip install), declaring the project version information and a complete list of dependencies (including asyncio>=3.9.0, io>=3.9.0, mypy>=1.16.0, tempfile>=3.9.0, ruff>=0.11.12, pytest>=8.3.5, etc., standard libraries), providing src/aiofiles/__init__.py as a unified API entry point, importing and exporting aioopen, wrap, stderr, stderr_bytes, stdin, stdin_bytes, stdout, stdout_bytes, and main functions, allowing users to access all main functions through simple \"from aiofiles import **\" and \"from aiofiles.threadpool import**\" statements.\n\n## Environment Configuration\n\n### Python Version\n\nThe Python version used in the current project is: Python 3.11.7\n\n### Versions of Core Dependent Libraries\n\n```Plain\niniconfig      2.1.0\npackaging      25.0\npip            23.2.1\npluggy         1.6.0\nPygments       2.19.2\npytest         8.4.1\npytest-asyncio 1.1.0\nsetuptools     65.5.1\nwheel          0.42.0\n```\n\n## Aiofiles Project Architecture\n\n### Project Directory Structure\n\n```Plain\nworkspace/\n├── .gitignore\n├── CHANGELOG.md\n├── Justfile\n├── LICENSE\n├── NOTICE\n├── README.md\n├── pyproject.toml\n├── src\n│   ├── aiofiles\n│   │   ├── __init__.py\n│   │   ├── base.py\n│   │   ├── os.py\n│   │   ├── ospath.py\n│   │   ├── tempfile\n│   │   │   ├── __init__.py\n│   │   │   ├── temptypes.py\n│   │   └── threadpool\n│   │       ├── __init__.py\n│   │       ├── binary.py\n│   │       ├── text.py\n│   │       └── utils.py\n├── tox.ini\n└── uv.lock\n\n```\n\n## API Usage Guide\n\n### Core API\n\n#### 1. Module Import\n\n```python\nfrom aiofiles.threadpool import open as aioopen\nimport aiofiles.threadpool\nfrom aiofiles.threadpool import wrap\nimport aiofiles.os\nfrom aiofiles import stderr, stderr_bytes, stdin, stdin_bytes, stdout, stdout_bytes\n```\n---\n\n\n\n### 2. AsyncBase Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AsyncBase class is the base class for all asynchronous file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\nclass AsyncBase:\n    def __init__(self, file, loop, executor):\n        self._file = file\n        self._executor = executor\n        self._ref_loop = loop\n\n    @property\n    def _loop(self):\n        return self._ref_loop or get_running_loop()\n\n    def __aiter__(self):\n        \"\"\"We are our own iterator.\"\"\"\n        return self\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return super().__repr__() + \" wrapping \" + repr(self._file)\n\n    async def __anext__(self):\n        \"\"\"Simulate normal file iteration.\n        Returns:\n            str: The next line of the file.\n        Raises:\n            StopAsyncIteration: If the end of the file is reached.\n        \"\"\"\n```\n\n### 3. AsyncIndirectBase Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AsyncIndirectBase class is the base class for all asynchronous indirect file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous indirect file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\nclass AsyncIndirectBase(AsyncBase):\n    def __init__(self, name, loop, executor, indirect):\n        self._indirect = indirect\n        self._name = name\n        super().__init__(None, loop, executor)\n\n    @property\n    def _file(self):\n        return self._indirect()\n\n    @_file.setter\n    def _file(self, v):\n        pass  # discard writes\n```\n\n### 4. AiofilesContextManager Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AiofilesContextManager class is the context manager for all asynchronous file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\nclass AiofilesContextManager(Awaitable, AbstractAsyncContextManager):\n    \"\"\"An adjusted async context manager for aiofiles.\"\"\"\n\n    __slots__ = (\"_coro\", \"_obj\")\n\n    def __init__(self, coro):\n        self._coro = coro\n        self._obj = None\n\n    def __await__(self):\n        \"\"\"Await the context manager.\n        Returns:\n            The object returned by the context manager.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def __aenter__(self):\n        return await self\n\n    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):\n        \"\"\"Exit the context manager.\n        Args:\n            exc_type: The type of the exception.\n            exc_val: The value of the exception.\n            exc_tb: The traceback of the exception.\n        \"\"\"\n```\n\n### 5. AsyncBufferedIOBase Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AsyncBufferedIOBase class is the base class for all asynchronous buffered file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous buffered file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\n@delegate_to_executor(\n    \"close\",\n    \"flush\",\n    \"isatty\",\n    \"read\",\n    \"read1\",\n    \"readinto\",\n    \"readline\",\n    \"readlines\",\n    \"seek\",\n    \"seekable\",\n    \"tell\",\n    \"truncate\",\n    \"writable\",\n    \"write\",\n    \"writelines\",\n)\n@proxy_method_directly(\"detach\", \"fileno\", \"readable\")\n@proxy_property_directly(\"closed\", \"raw\", \"name\", \"mode\")\nclass AsyncBufferedIOBase(AsyncBase):\n    \"\"\"The asyncio executor version of io.BufferedWriter and BufferedIOBase.\"\"\"\n```\n\n### 6. AsyncBufferedReader Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AsyncBufferedReader class is the reader for all asynchronous buffered file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous buffered file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\n@delegate_to_executor(\"peek\")\nclass AsyncBufferedReader(AsyncBufferedIOBase):\n    \"\"\"The asyncio executor version of io.BufferedReader and Random.\"\"\"\n```\n\n### 7. function, supporting text and binary modes, and providing an asynchronous interface similar to the standard file API.\n\n**Core Functions**:\n- Asynchronous file opening (`aiofiles.open`)\n- Support for all standard file modes (r, w, a, rb, wb, ab, r+, w+, rb+, wb+, ab+)\n- Support for asynchronous context manager (`async with`)\n- Basic file operations (read, write, close)\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File path and mode\nimport aiofiles\n\n# Asynchronous reading of a text file\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    content = await f.read()\n    # It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous spooled temporary file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\n@delegate_to_executor(\"fileno\", \"rollover\")\n@cond_delegate_to_executor(\n    \"close\",\n    \"flush\",\n    \"isatty\",\n    \"read\",\n    \"readline\",\n    \"readlines\",\n    \"seek\",\n    \"tell\",\n    \"truncate\",\n)\n@proxy_property_directly(\"closed\", \"encoding\", \"mode\", \"name\", \"newlines\")\nclass AsyncSpooledTemporaryFile(AsyncBase):\n    \"\"\"Async wrapper for SpooledTemporaryFile class\"\"\"\n\n    async def _check(self):\n        \"\"\"Check if the file is rolled.\n        Returns:\n            bool: True if the file is rolled, False otherwise.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def write(self, s):\n        \"\"\"Implementation to anticipate rollover.\n        Args:\n            s: The string to write to the file.\n        Returns:\n            int: The number of bytes written.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def writelines(self, iterable):\n        \"\"\"Implementation to anticipate rollover.\n        Args:\n            iterable: The iterable to write to the file.\n        Returns:\n            int: The number of bytes written.\n```\n\n### 14. function, supporting text and binary modes, and providing an asynchronous interface similar to the standard file API.\n\n**Core Functions**:\n- Asynchronous file opening (`aiofiles.open`)\n- Support Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File path and mode\nimport aiofiles\n\n# Asynchronous reading of a text file\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    content = await f.read()\n    # Interfaces**:\n- `test_simple.py`: Test basic file operations\n- `tests/threadpool/test_open.py`: Test the file opening function\n\n### Node 2: Async File Reading Operations\n\n**Function Description**: Implement asynchronous file pointer positioning, file attribute query, and file status checking functions.\n\n**Core Functions**:\n- `seek()`: Move the file pointer\n- `tell()`: Get the current file position\n- `fileno()`: Get the file descriptor (synchronous method, not async)\n- `isatty()`: Check if it is a terminal device\n- `seekable()`: Check if positioning is supported\n- `readable()`: Check if readable (synchronous method, not async)\n- `writable()`: Check if writable\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File object and positioning parameters\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r+') as f:\n    # Get the current position\n    pos = await f.tell()\n    # Output: Current file position (integer)\n    \n    # Move to a specified position\n    await f.seek(10)\n    # Output: None\n    \n    # Move to the end of the file\n    await f.seek(0, 2)\n    # Output: None\n    \n    # Check file attributes\n    is_seekable = await f.seekable()\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    is_readable = f.readable()  # Synchronous method, not async\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    is_writable = await f.writable()\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    is_tty = await f.isatty()\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    fd = f.fileno()  # Synchronous method, not async\n    # Output: File descriptor (integer)\n```\n\n**Test Interfaces**:\n- handling\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File path\nimport aiofiles\n\n# Asynchronous context manager\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    # Automatically handle file opening and closing\n    content = await f.read()\n    # Output: File content, file automatically closed\n\n# Asynchronous iterator\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    async for line in f:\n        # Process file content line by line\n        print(line.strip())\n        # Output: Each line of content (without newline character)\n\n# Exception-safe handling\ntry:\n    async with aiofiles.open('nonexistent.txt', 'r') as f:\n        content = await f.read()\nexcept FileNotFoundError:\n    # Automatically close resources when the file does not exist\n    print(\"File not found\")\n```\n\n**Test Interfaces**:\n- `test_simple.py`: Test the asynchronous context manager\n- `tests/threadpool/test_text.py`: Test the asynchronous iterator\n\n### Node 6: This summary carries forward to the next task.\n\n--- BEGIN SPECIFICATION ---\n## Introduction and Goals of the aiofiles Project\n\naiofiles is a Python library **for asynchronous file operations**, providing asynchronous processing capabilities for local disk files in asyncio applications. This library is licensed under the Apache 2 license. It delegates blocking file I/O operations to separate threads through a thread pool executor, thus avoiding blocking the asyncio event loop. Its core features include: **asynchronous file operation interfaces** (providing asynchronous file APIs very similar to Python's standard blocking APIs), **support for various file types** (including buffered and unbuffered binary files, buffered text files), **support for asynchronous iteration** (supporting async for loops for file line iteration), and an **asynchronous temporary file module** (providing asynchronous interfaces such as TemporaryFile, NamedTemporaryFile, SpooledTemporaryFile, and TemporaryDirectory).\n\nIn short, aiofiles aims to solve the problem of file I/O blocking in asyncio applications. By delegating file operations to a thread pool executor, developers can safely perform file read and write operations in asynchronous applications without interfering with the execution of the event loop. This library supports Python 3.9 and above, providing a complete solution for asynchronous file operations, including file opening, reading and writing, directory operations, and temporary file handling. It is an important tool for building high-performance asynchronous applications.\n\n## Natural Language Instruction (Prompt)\n\nPlease create a Python project named aiofiles to implement an asynchronous file operation library. The project should include the following features:\n\n1. Core of asynchronous file operations: Implement asynchronous file opening, reading, and writing functions, providing asynchronous interfaces similar to Python's standard file APIs. It should support asynchronous operations on text and binary files, including asynchronous versions of methods such as read(), write(), readline(), readlines(), and writelines().\n\n2. Integration of thread pool executor: Delegate blocking file I/O operations to separate threads through asyncio's thread pool executor to ensure that file operations do not block the event loop. Implement an asynchronous wrapper for file operations, supporting custom configuration of loop and executor parameters.\n\n3. Support for asynchronous iteration: Implement the iteration function of asynchronous file objects, supporting async for loops for file line iteration. File objects should implement the asynchronous iterator protocol, allowing asynchronous line-by-line reading of file contents.\n\n4. Asynchronous temporary file module: Implement the functions of the tempfile module in an asynchronous version, including classes such as TemporaryFile, NamedTemporaryFile, SpooledTemporaryFile, and TemporaryDirectory. Each class should support the async with context manager, providing a complete asynchronous temporary file operation interface.\n\n5. Asynchronous OS operations: Implement asynchronous versions of file system operations, including file status query (stat), directory operations (mkdir, makedirs, rmdir, removedirs), file movement (rename, renames, replace), file deletion (remove, unlink), symbolic link operations (symlink, readlink, link), and directory traversal (listdir, scandir). All operations should be made asynchronous through the thread pool executor.\n\n6. Asynchronous standard input and output: Provide interfaces for asynchronous access to the system's standard input and output, including stdin, stdout, stderr, and their corresponding byte versions (stdin_bytes, stdout_bytes, stderr_bytes), supporting asynchronous reading and writing of standard streams.\n\n7. Interface design: Design clear API interfaces for each functional module, supporting the async with context manager and asynchronous iterators (async for). Each module should define clear input and output formats and error handling mechanisms.\n\n8. Examples and tests: Provide complete example code and test cases to demonstrate how to use aiofiles.open() for asynchronous file operations, how to use asynchronous temporary files, and how to perform asynchronous file system operations. Tests should cover various file types and operation scenarios.\n\n9. Core file requirements: The project must include a complete pyproject.toml file, configuring the project as an installable package (supporting pip install), declaring the project version information and a complete list of dependencies (including asyncio>=3.9.0, io>=3.9.0, mypy>=1.16.0, tempfile>=3.9.0, ruff>=0.11.12, pytest>=8.3.5, etc., standard libraries), providing src/aiofiles/__init__.py as a unified API entry point, importing and exporting aioopen, wrap, stderr, stderr_bytes, stdin, stdin_bytes, stdout, stdout_bytes, and main functions, allowing users to access all main functions through simple \"from aiofiles import **\" and \"from aiofiles.threadpool import**\" statements.\n\n## Environment Configuration\n\n### Python Version\n\nThe Python version used in the current project is: Python 3.11.7\n\n### Versions of Core Dependent Libraries\n\n```Plain\niniconfig      2.1.0\npackaging      25.0\npip            23.2.1\npluggy         1.6.0\nPygments       2.19.2\npytest         8.4.1\npytest-asyncio 1.1.0\nsetuptools     65.5.1\nwheel          0.42.0\n```\n\n## Aiofiles Project Architecture\n\n### Project Directory Structure\n\n```Plain\nworkspace/\n├── .gitignore\n├── CHANGELOG.md\n├── Justfile\n├── LICENSE\n├── NOTICE\n├── README.md\n├── pyproject.toml\n├── src\n│   ├── aiofiles\n│   │   ├── __init__.py\n│   │   ├── base.py\n│   │   ├── os.py\n│   │   ├── ospath.py\n│   │   ├── tempfile\n│   │   │   ├── __init__.py\n│   │   │   ├── temptypes.py\n│   │   └── threadpool\n│   │       ├── __init__.py\n│   │       ├── binary.py\n│   │       ├── text.py\n│   │       └── utils.py\n├── tox.ini\n└── uv.lock\n\n```\n\n## API Usage Guide\n\n### Core API\n\n#### 1. Module Import\n\n```python\nfrom aiofiles.threadpool import open as aioopen\nimport aiofiles.threadpool\nfrom aiofiles.threadpool import wrap\nimport aiofiles.os\nfrom aiofiles import stderr, stderr_bytes, stdin, stdin_bytes, stdout, stdout_bytes\n```\n---\n\n\n\n### 2. AsyncBase Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AsyncBase class is the base class for all asynchronous file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\nclass AsyncBase:\n    def __init__(self, file, loop, executor):\n        self._file = file\n        self._executor = executor\n        self._ref_loop = loop\n\n    @property\n    def _loop(self):\n        return self._ref_loop or get_running_loop()\n\n    def __aiter__(self):\n        \"\"\"We are our own iterator.\"\"\"\n        return self\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return super().__repr__() + \" wrapping \" + repr(self._file)\n\n    async def __anext__(self):\n        \"\"\"Simulate normal file iteration.\n        Returns:\n            str: The next line of the file.\n        Raises:\n            StopAsyncIteration: If the end of the file is reached.\n        \"\"\"\n```\n\n### 3. AsyncIndirectBase Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AsyncIndirectBase class is the base class for all asynchronous indirect file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous indirect file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\nclass AsyncIndirectBase(AsyncBase):\n    def __init__(self, name, loop, executor, indirect):\n        self._indirect = indirect\n        self._name = name\n        super().__init__(None, loop, executor)\n\n    @property\n    def _file(self):\n        return self._indirect()\n\n    @_file.setter\n    def _file(self, v):\n        pass  # discard writes\n```\n\n### 4. AiofilesContextManager Class\n\n**Class Description**: The AiofilesContextManager class is the context manager for all asynchronous file operations. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\nclass AiofilesContextManager(Awaitable, AbstractAsyncContextManager):\n    \"\"\"An adjusted async context manager for aiofiles.\"\"\"\n\n    __slots__ = (\"_coro\", \"_obj\")\n\n    def __init__(self, coro):\n        self._coro = coro\n        self._obj = None\n\n    def __await__(self):\n        \"\"\"Await the context manager.\n        Returns:\n            The object returned by the context manager.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def __aenter__(self):\n        return await self\n\n    async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc_val, exc_tb):\n        \"\"\"Exit the context manager.\n        Args:\n            exc_type: The type of the exception.\n            exc_val: The value of the exception.\n            exc_tb: The traceback of the exception.\n        \"\"\"\n```\n\n### 5. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous spooled temporary file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\n@delegate_to_executor(\"fileno\", \"rollover\")\n@cond_delegate_to_executor(\n    \"close\",\n    \"flush\",\n    \"isatty\",\n    \"read\",\n    \"readline\",\n    \"readlines\",\n    \"seek\",\n    \"tell\",\n    \"truncate\",\n)\n@proxy_property_directly(\"closed\", \"encoding\", \"mode\", \"name\", \"newlines\")\nclass AsyncSpooledTemporaryFile(AsyncBase):\n    \"\"\"Async wrapper for SpooledTemporaryFile class\"\"\"\n\n    async def _check(self):\n        \"\"\"Check if the file is rolled.\n        Returns:\n            bool: True if the file is rolled, False otherwise.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def write(self, s):\n        \"\"\"Implementation to anticipate rollover.\n        Args:\n            s: The string to write to the file.\n        Returns:\n            int: The number of bytes written.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def writelines(self, iterable):\n        \"\"\"Implementation to anticipate rollover.\n        Args:\n            iterable: The iterable to write to the file.\n        Returns:\n            int: The number of bytes written.\n```\n\n### 14. It provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous spooled temporary file operations.\n\n**Class Definition**:\n```python\n@delegate_to_executor(\"fileno\", \"rollover\")\n@cond_delegate_to_executor(\n    \"close\",\n    \"flush\",\n    \"isatty\",\n    \"read\",\n    \"readline\",\n    \"readlines\",\n    \"seek\",\n    \"tell\",\n    \"truncate\",\n)\n@proxy_property_directly(\"closed\", \"encoding\", \"mode\", \"name\", \"newlines\")\nclass AsyncSpooledTemporaryFile(AsyncBase):\n    \"\"\"Async wrapper for SpooledTemporaryFile class\"\"\"\n\n    async def _check(self):\n        \"\"\"Check if the file is rolled.\n        Returns:\n            bool: True if the file is rolled, False otherwise.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def write(self, s):\n        \"\"\"Implementation to anticipate rollover.\n        Args:\n            s: The string to write to the file.\n        Returns:\n            int: The number of bytes written.\n        \"\"\"\n\n    async def writelines(self, iterable):\n        \"\"\"Implementation to anticipate rollover.\n        Args:\n            iterable: The iterable to write to the file.\n        Returns:\n            int: The number of bytes written.\n```\n\n### 14. function, supporting text and binary modes, and providing an asynchronous interface similar to the standard file API.\n\n**Core Functions**:\n- Asynchronous file opening (`aiofiles.open`)\n- Support for all standard file modes (r, w, a, rb, wb, ab, r+, w+, rb+, wb+, ab+)\n- Support for asynchronous context manager (`async with`)\n- Basic file operations (read, write, close)\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File path and mode\nimport aiofiles\n\n# Asynchronous reading of a text file\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    content = await f.read()\n    # Interfaces**:\n- `test_simple.py`: Test basic file operations\n- `tests/threadpool/test_open.py`: Test the file opening function\n\n### Node 2: Async File Reading Operations\n\n**Function FileIO)\n- `peek()`: Preview data without moving the file pointer (only available for BufferedReader)\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File object and reading parameters\nasync   partial = await f.read(100)\n    # Output: String of at most 100 characters\n    \n    # Read line by line\n    line = await f.readline()\n    # Output: Single-line string (including newline character)\n    \n    # Read all lines\n    lines = await f.readlines()\n    # Output: List of strings, one element per line\n\n# Reading a binary file\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.bin', 'rb') as `tests/threadpool/test_binary.py`: Test binary file writing\n- `tests/threadpool/test_text.py`: Test text file writing\n\n### Node 4: Async File Positioning and Attribute Operations\n\n**Function Description**: Implement asynchronous file pointer positioning, file attribute query, and file status checking functions.\n\n**Core Functions**:\n- `seek()`: Move the file pointer\n- `tell()`: Get the current file position\n- `fileno()`: Get the file descriptor (synchronous method, not async)\n- `isatty()`: Check if it is a terminal device\n- `seekable()`: Check if positioning is supported\n- `readable()`: Check if readable (synchronous method, not async)\n- `writable()`: Check if writable\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File object and positioning parameters\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r+') as f:\n    # Get the current position\n    pos = await f.tell()\n    # Output: Current file position (integer)\n    \n    # Move to a specified position\n    await f.seek(10)\n    # Output: None\n    \n    # Move to the end of the file\n    await f.seek(0, 2)\n    # Output: None\n    \n    # Check file attributes\n    is_seekable = await f.seekable()\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    is_readable = f.readable()  # Synchronous method, not async\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    is_writable = await f.writable()\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    is_tty = await f.isatty()\n    # Output: True/False\n    \n    fd = f.fileno()  # Synchronous method, not async\n    # Output: File descriptor (integer)\n```\n\n**Test Interfaces**:\n- `tests/threadpool/test_binary.py`: Test binary file positioning and attributes\n- `tests/threadpool/test_text.py`: Test text file positioning and attributes\n\n### Node 5: Async File Iteration and handling\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: File path\nimport aiofiles\n\n# Asynchronous context manager\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    # Automatically handle file opening and closing\n    content = await f.read()\n    # Output: File content, file automatically closed\n\n# Asynchronous iterator\nasync with aiofiles.open('test.txt', 'r') as f:\n    async for line in f:\n        # Process file content line by line\n        print(line.strip())\n        # Output: Each line of content (without newline character)\n\n# Exception-safe handling\ntry:\n    async with aiofiles.open('nonexistent.txt', 'r') as f:\n        content = await f.read()\nexcept FileNotFoundError:\n    # Automatically close resources when the file does not exist\n    print(\"File not found\")\n```\n\n**Test Interfaces**:\n- `test_simple.py`: Test the asynchronous context manager\n- `tests/threadpool/test_text.py`: Test the asynchronous iterator\n\n### Node 6: Async Temporary File Management\n\n**Function Description**: Implement the functions of asynchronous temporary file creation and management, including various types of temporary files and directories.\n\n**Core Functions**:\n- `TemporaryFile()`: Create a temporary file\n- `NamedTemporaryFile()`: Create a named temporary file\n- `SpooledTemporaryFile()`: Create a memory-buffered temporary file\n- `TemporaryDirectory()`: Create a temporary directory\n- Support for deletion policy configuration (delete parameter)\n- Support for max_size parameter in SpooledTemporaryFile (controls when to rollover to disk)\n\n**Input and Output Examples**:\n```python\n# Input: Temporary file parameters\nimport aiofiles.tempfile\n\n# Create a temporary file\nasync with aiofiles.tempfile.TemporaryFile() as f:\n    await f.write(b'temporary data')\n    await f.seek(0)\n    data = await f.read()\n    # Output: Content of the temporary file, file automatically deleted\n\n# Create a named temporary file\nasync with aiofiles.tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(delete=False) as f:\n    filename = f.name\n    await f.write(b'named temp data')\n    # Output: Path of the temporary file, file retained\n\n# Create a memory-buffered temporary file\nasync with aiofiles.tempfile.SpooledTemporaryFile(max_size=1024) as f:\n    await f.write(b'small data')\n    # Output: Data in memory, written to disk when the size limit is exceeded\n\n# Create a temporary directory\nasync with aiofiles.tempfile.TemporaryDirectory() as temp_dir:\n    file_path = os.path.join(temp_dir, \"test.txt\")\n    async with aiofiles.open(file_path, 'w') as f:\n        await f.write('test content')\n    # Output: Path of the temporary directory, directory automatically deleted\n```\n\n**Test index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..f393bb1823eb2482e356fd7a21d9210c1c163922

+++ b/pyproject.toml
@@ -0,0 +1,50 @@
+[build-system]
+requires = ["setuptools>=65.5.1", "wheel>=0.42.0"]
+build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"
+
+[project]
+name = "aiofiles"
+version = "24.1.0"
+description = "Asynchronous file access for Python asyncio"
+readme = "README.md"
+license = {text = "Apache-2.0"}
+requires-python = ">=3.9"
+authors = [
+    {name = "Tin Tvrtkovic", email = "tinchester@gmail.com"},
+]
+classifiers = [
+    "Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable",
+    "License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License",
+    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
+    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.9",
+    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.10",
+    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11",
+    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12",
+    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13",
+    "Topic :: System :: Filesystems",
+    "Framework :: AsyncIO",
+]
+
+[project.optional-dependencies]
+dev = [
+    "mypy>=1.16.0",
+    "ruff>=0.11.12",
+    "pytest>=8.3.5",
+    "pytest-asyncio>=1.1.0",
+]
+
+[tool.setuptools.packages.find]
+where = ["src"]
+
+[tool.pytest.ini_options]
+asyncio_mode = "auto"
+testpaths = ["tests"]
+
+[tool.ruff]
+line-length = 120
+target-version = "py39"
+
+[tool.mypy]
+python_version = "3.9"
+warn_return_any = true
+warn_unused_configs = true

new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..8696e7663f45ddc75bca55b810e12e313a000896
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/aiofiles.egg-info/PKG-INFO
@@ -0,0 +1,44 @@
+Metadata-Version: 2.4
+Name: aiofiles
+Version: 24.1.0
+Summary: Asynchronous file access for Python asyncio
+Author-email: Tin Tvrtkovic <tinchester@gmail.com>
+License: Apache-2.0
+Classifier: Development Status :: 5 - Production/Stable
+Classifier: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License
+Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3
+Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3.9
+Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3.10
+Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11
+Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3.12
+Classifier: Programming Language :: Python :: 3.13
+Classifier: Topic :: System :: Filesystems
+Classifier: Framework :: AsyncIO
+Requires-Python: >=3.9
+Description-Content-Type: text/markdown
+Provides-Extra: dev
+Requires-Dist: mypy>=1.16.0; extra == "dev"
+Requires-Dist: ruff>=0.11.12; extra == "dev"
+Requires-Dist: pytest>=8.3.5; extra == "dev"
+Requires-Dist: pytest-asyncio>=1.1.0; extra == "dev"
+
+# aiofiles
+
+Asynchronous file access for Python asyncio.
+
+## Installation
+
+```bash
+pip install aiofiles
+```
+
+## Usage
+
+```python
+import aiofiles
+
+async def read_file():
+    async with aiofiles.open('test.txt', mode='r') as f:
+        contents = await f.read()
+        return contents
+```

new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..5df2c044287a7d3d24e80a178c54539f2431c11e
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/aiofiles/__init__.py
@@ -0,0 +1,25 @@
+"""
+aiofiles - Asynchronous file operations for Python.
+
+This module provides asynchronous file operation interfaces, delegating
+blocking file I/O operations to a thread pool executor to avoid blocking
+the asyncio event loop.
+"""
+
+from aiofiles.threadpool import open
+from aiofiles.threadpool import wrap
+from aiofiles.threadpool import stdin, stdout, stderr
+from aiofiles.threadpool import stdin_bytes, stdout_bytes, stderr_bytes
+import aiofiles.tempfile as tempfile
+
+__all__ = [
+    "open",
+    "wrap",
+    "tempfile",
+    "stdin",
+    "stdout",
+    "stderr",
+    "stdin_bytes",
+    "stdout_bytes",
+    "stderr_bytes",
+]

diff --git a/src/aiofiles/base.py b/src/aiofiles/base.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..206487907efe594cab4fc82d9261663ab5736621
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/aiofiles/base.py
@@ -0,0 +1,162 @@
+"""Base classes and utilities for aiofiles asynchronous file operations."""
+
+import asyncio
+from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
+from functools import wraps
+
+from types import TracebackType
+from typing import Any, Awaitable, Optional, Type
+
+
+class AsyncBase:
+    """Base class for all asynchronous file operations.
+    
+    Provides the basic functionality for all asynchronous file operations,
+    including async iteration support.
+    """
+
+    def __init__(self, file: Any, loop: Optional[Any] = None, executor: Optional[Any] = None):
+        self._file = file
+        self._executor = executor
+        self._ref_loop = loop
+
+    @property
+    def _loop(self) -> asyncio.AbstractEventLoop:
+        """Get the event loop, using the stored reference or the currently running loop."""
+        return self._ref_loop or asyncio.get_running_loop()
+
+    def __aiter__(self):
+        """We are our own iterator."""
+        return self
+
+    def __repr__(self) -> str:
+        return super().__repr__() + " wrapping " + repr(self._file)
+
+    async def __anext__(self) -> str:
+        """Simulate normal file iteration.
+        
+        Returns:
+            str: The next line of the file.
+        
+        Raises:
+            StopAsyncIteration: If the end of the file is reached.
+        """
+        line = await self.readline()
+        if line:
+            return line
+        raise StopAsyncIteration
+
+
+class AsyncIndirectBase(AsyncBase):
+    """Base class for all asynchronous indirect file operations.
+    
+    Used for file objects that may be replaced over time (e.g., SpooledTemporaryFile).
+    The underlying file is obtained via an indirect callable.
+    """
+
+    def __init__(self, name: str, loop: Optional[Any] = None, executor: Optional[Any] = None, indirect: Optional[Any] = None):
+        self._indirect = indirect
+        self._name = name
+        super().__init__(None, loop, executor)
+
+    @property
+    def _file(self) -> Any:
+        """Get the current file object via the indirect callable."""
+        return self._indirect()
+
+    @_file.setter
+    def _file(self, v: Any) -> None:
+        """Discard writes to _file."""
+        pass  # discard writes
+
+
+class AiofilesContextManager:
+    """An async context manager for aiofiles.
+    
+    Wraps a coroutine that returns a file-like object, allowing it to be
+    used with async with statements.
+    """
+
+    __slots__ = ("_coro", "_obj")
+
+    def __init__(self, coro: Awaitable):
+        self._coro = coro
+        self._obj: Optional[Any] = None
+
+    def __await__(self):
+        """Await the context manager.
+        
+        Returns:
+            The object returned by the coroutine.
+        """
+        self._obj = yield from self._coro
+        return self._obj
+
+    async def __aenter__(self) -> Any:
+        """Enter the context manager.
+        
+        Returns:
+            The file-like object from the coroutine.
+        """
+        self._obj = await self._coro
+        return self._obj
+
+    async def __aexit__(
+        self,
+        exc_type: Optional[Type[BaseException]],
+        exc_val: Optional[BaseException],
+        exc_tb: Optional[TracebackType],
+    ) -> None:
+        """Exit the context manager.
+        
+        Args:
+            exc_type: The type of the exception.
+            exc_val: The value of the exception.
+            exc_tb: The traceback of the exception.
+        """
+        if self._obj is not None:
+            await self._obj.close()
+            # Close the wrapper reference if present (for NamedTemporaryFile)
+            wrapper = getattr(self._obj, "_wrapper_ref", None)
+            if wrapper is not None:
+                wrapper.close()
+
+
+def wrap(func):
+    """Wrap a synchronous function to run in a thread pool executor asynchronously.
+    
+    This decorator converts a blocking function into an async function by
+    delegating its execution to a thread pool executor, preventing it from
+    blocking the asyncio event loop.
+    
+    Args:
+        func: The synchronous function to wrap.
+    
+    Returns:
+        An async wrapper function that executes the original function in a thread pool.
+    """
+    @wraps(func)
+    async def run(*args, loop=None, executor=None, **kwargs):
+        """Wrap the function.
+        
+        Args:
+            func: The function to wrap.
+            args: The arguments to pass to the function.
+            loop: The event loop to use.
+            executor: The executor to use.
+            kwargs: The keyword arguments to pass to the function.
+        
+        Returns:
+            The result of the wrapped function call.
+        """
+        if loop is None:
+            loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
+        
+        if executor is None:
+            executor = ThreadPoolExecutor()
+        
+        return await loop.run_in_executor(
+            executor,
+            lambda: func(*args, **kwargs),
+        )
+    return run
diff --git a/src/aiofiles/os/__init__.py b/src/aiofiles/os/__init__.py
new file mode 100644
index 0000000000000000000000000000000000000000..b7f7e9a33d5c561de3d70923bca698c35d6766d2
--- /dev/null
+++ b/src/aiofiles/os/__init__.py
@@ -0,0 +1,98 @@
+"""Asynchronous OS file system operations for aiofiles.
+
+This module provides async versions of common os module functions,
+delegating blocking I/O operations to a thread pool executor.
+"""
+
+import os
+import sys
+
+from aiofiles.base import wrap as wrap_func
+
+
+# Re-export os.path as path
+path = os.path
+
+
+__all__ = [
+    "path",
+    "stat",
+    "rename",
+    "renames",
+    "replace",
+    "remove",
+    "unlink",
+    "mkdir",
+    "makedirs",
+    "rmdir",
+    "removedirs",
+    "symlink",
+    "readlink",
+    "link",
+    "listdir",
+    "scandir",
+    "access",
+    "wrap",
+    "getcwd",
+]
+
+
+# File status query
+stat = wrap_func(os.stat)
+
+
+# File movement
+rename = wrap_func(os.rename)
+renames = wrap_func(os.renames)
+replace = wrap_func(os.replace)
+
+
+# File deletion
+remove = wrap_func(os.remove)
+unlink = wrap_func(os.unlink)
+
+
+# Directory operations
+mkdir = wrap_func(os.mkdir)
+makedirs = wrap_func(os.makedirs)
+rmdir = wrap_func(os.rmdir)
+removedirs = wrap_func(os.removedirs)
+
+
+# Symbolic link operations
+symlink = wrap_func(os.symlink)
+readlink = wrap_func(os.readlink)
+
+# Hard link (not available on all platforms)
+if hasattr(os, "link"):
+    link = wrap_func(os.link)
+else:
+    link = None  # type: ignore[assignment]
+
+
+# Directory traversal
+listdir = wrap_func(os.listdir)
+scandir = wrap_func(os.scandir)
+
+
+# File access check
+access = wrap_func(os.access)
+
+
+# Current working directory
+getcwd = wrap_func(os.getcwd)
+
+
+def wrap(func):
+    """Wrap a synchronous os function to run asynchronously via a thread pool.
+
+    This is a convenience wrapper equivalent to the base.wrap decorator,
+    provided here for os-specific usage.
+
+    Args:
+        func: The synchronous function to wrap.
+
+    Returns:
+        An async wrapper that executes the function in a thread pool.
+    """
+    return wrap_func(func)

+"""
+Async versions of os.path functions.
+
+All functions delegate blocking os.path operations to a thread pool executor
+to avoid blocking the asyncio event loop.
+"""
+
+import os
+
+from aiofiles.base import wrap
+
+__all__ = [
+    "abspath",
+    "getatime",
+    "getctime",
+    "getmtime",
+    "getsize",
+    "exists",
+    "isdir",
+    "isfile",
+    "islink",
+    "ismount",
+    "samefile",
+    "sameopenfile",
+]
+
+# Async version of os.path.abspath
+abspath = wrap(os.path.abspath)
+
+# Async version of os.path.exists
+exists = wrap(os.path.exists)
+
+# Async version of os.path.isfile
+isfile = wrap(os.path.isfile)
+
+# Async version of os.path.isdir
+isdir = wrap(os.path.isdir)
+
+# Async version of os.path.islink
+islink = wrap(os.path.islink)
+
+# Async version of os.path.ismount
+ismount = wrap(os.path.ismount)
+
+# Async version of os.path.getsize
+getsize = wrap(os.path.getsize)
+
+# Async version of os.path.getatime
+getatime = wrap(os.path.getatime)
+
+# Async version of os.path.getctime
+getctime = wrap(os.path.getctime)
+
+# Async version of os.path.getmtime
+getmtime = wrap(os.path.getmtime)
+
+# Async version of os.path.samefile
+samefile = wrap(os.path.samefile)
+
+# Async version of os.path.sameopenfile
+sameopenfile = wrap(os.path.sameopenfile)
